text:
Abstract: Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) are a group of man-made compounds that have been linked to serious health and environmental risks. Even at ultra trace (ng·L⁻¹) levels, the adverse effects from these chemicals have been shown to remain present. There is an urgent need for on-site monitoring of water distribution systems and other infrastructure to inform management decisions, but current methods are not suitable for field use due to their bulky nature and laboratory-based setup. This article presents a novel method for on-site, field-deployable sensing of PFOA and PFOS at the parts-per-trillion level using amplifying fluorescent polymers (AFPs).

We have developed advanced functionalized polymers (AFPs) to develop a sensitive analytical method for the detection of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) in aqueous samples. The AFPs are based on highly fluorinated poly($p$-phenylene ethynylene) (PPE) and polyfluorene (PF) backbones functionalized with pyridine-based selectors. The fluorescent analyte probe (FAP) detection principle is based on proton transfer from the acidic PFAS to pyridine sites. The highly fluorinated polymer chains enable a hydrophobic interaction with the hydrophobic acid of the PFAS resulting in extraction of the analyte from the aqueous phase to polymer. Once the PFAS molecules interact with the polymer backbone the protonation of the pyridine creates low-energy exciton traps and result in a red shift in the fluorescence emission. The AFPs performance was examined in both film and nanoparticle (CPdot) format and the films showed a limit of detection of approximately 1 ppb of PFAS solution, while the nanoparticles showed a limit of detection of 100 ppt. It was observed that the fluorescent probe detection principle was not affected by various aqueous matrices such as MilliQ, DI and well water samples. Therefore, the AFPs-based analysis method demonstrates a high sensitivity towards real time field analysis of aqueous PFAS samples.
